title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe (Mole Negro)
seo.metaDescription: Master the rich, complex flavors of Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with our authentic recipe. Discover step-by-step instructions to create this traditional Mexican culinary masterpiece at home.
longTailKeywords: how to make Oaxacan Black Mole
Oaxacan Black Mole step by step
best Mole Negro recipe at home
seo.h1: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?
Ingredients You Need for Mole Negro
Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Mole Negro
Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole
seo.imageAlt: A vibrant bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with tender chicken, rice, and a sprinkle of sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Dive into the rich, smoky flavors of Oaxacan Black Mole, a truly iconic Mexican dish perfect for your next special dinner.

description: Mole Negro is a culinary masterpiece, a deep, rich sauce from Oaxaca that embodies the heart of Mexican cuisine. Its complex flavors come from a symphony of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and a touch of chocolate, creating an unforgettable gastronomic experience perfect for special occasions. This authentic recipe guides you through creating this iconic black mole, balancing smoky, sweet, and savory notes for a truly memorable dish.

instructions.title: Toast and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Briefly toast the dried mulato, ancho, and pasilla chiles in a dry comal or heavy skillet over medium heat for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. Transfer the toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 15-20 minutes until soft. Drain the chiles, reserving about 1 cup of the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Roast Vegetables and Aromatics
instructions.text: On the same comal or skillet, roast the halved Roma tomatoes, tomatillos, roughly chopped white onion, and peeled garlic cloves until they are softened and slightly charred in spots. This should take about 8-10 minutes. Set aside to cool slightly.

instructions.title: Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In a small pan, heat 1 tablespoon of lard or vegetable oil over medium heat. Add the almonds, pecans, sesame seeds, raisins, cinnamon stick, whole cloves, black peppercorns, cumin seeds, Mexican oregano, and thyme. Toast for about 3-4 minutes, stirring constantly, until fragrant. Add the sliced ripe plantain and fry for another 1-2 minutes until golden brown. Remove from heat.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted vegetables (tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, garlic), and the toasted nuts, seeds, spices, and plantain into a blender. Add 1 cup of chicken broth and about 1/2 cup of the reserved chile soaking liquid. Blend until the mixture is very smooth. You may need to do this in batches, adding more chicken broth or chile liquid as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency.

instructions.title: Strain and Simmer the Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 1 tablespoon of lard or vegetable oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pass the blended mole mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into the hot oil, pressing down firmly with a sturdy spatula or ladle to extract all the liquid and pulp. Discard any remaining solids in the sieve. Bring the strained mole sauce to a gentle simmer, stirring frequently to prevent it from sticking to the bottom of the pot. Let it cook for about 10-15 minutes.

instructions.title: Finish the Mole and Serve
instructions.text: Stir in the chopped Mexican chocolate into the simmering mole sauce until it is completely melted and incorporated. Season the mole with sugar or piloncillo and salt to taste, adjusting for your preferred balance of sweet and savory. Continue to simmer the mole for another 5-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it has thickened to your desired consistency and the flavors have fully melded. Serve hot over cooked chicken, turkey, or pork, accompanied by rice and warm tortillas.

tips: To achieve the deepest black color and flavor, ensure your chiles are toasted, not burnt. A slight char is good, but intense burning will make the mole bitter.
For an even smoother mole, after blending, you can pass the mixture through a traditional molino (grinder) or a finer sieve multiple times.
Mole Negro improves with age. Make it a day ahead and gently reheat it before serving to allow the complex flavors to fully develop and meld.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Mexico with one of its most revered dishes: Mole Negro. This isn't just a sauce; it's a symphony of flavors, a labor of love, and a cornerstone of Oaxacan cuisine, perfect for a truly special Mexican dinner recipe. Often called the "king of moles," Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is an incredibly complex, rich, and deeply satisfying dish that embodies centuries of culinary tradition. While its ingredient list might seem daunting and its preparation lengthy, the reward is an unparalleled gastronomic experience that will leave a lasting impression on your palate and your guests. Get ready to dive deep into the magic behind this iconic black mole.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

Mole, pronounced "MOH-lay," is a generic term for a variety of sauces in Mexican cuisine, many of which are famously complex. Among these, Mole Negro, or Oaxacan Black Mole, stands as a testament to the intricate culinary heritage of Oaxaca, a state renowned for its seven distinct moles. What sets Mole Negro apart is its signature deep, almost obsidian color, achieved through a careful charring of a specific blend of chiles and other ingredients.

The flavor profile of Oaxacan Black Mole is a masterful balance of smoky, sweet, savory, earthy, and subtly spicy notes. It typically includes a staggering number of ingredients—often over 20, sometimes as many as 30 or more—such as various types of dried chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, aromatics, and a touch of Mexican chocolate. This intricate blend creates a sauce that is velvety smooth, profoundly aromatic, and incredibly rich. It’s a dish reserved for celebrations, holidays, and significant family gatherings, a true centerpiece for any authentic Mexican dinner recipe.

## Ingredients You Need for Mole Negro

Creating an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole is an art form, and the quality and variety of your ingredients are paramount. Here's a comprehensive list of what you'll typically need to assemble this magnificent sauce:

*   **The Chiles (the heart of the mole):**
    *   6-8 large mulato chiles
    *   6-8 large ancho chiles
    *   6-8 large pasilla chiles
    *   *Optional but traditional:* 2-3 chilhuacle negro chiles (these are rare and expensive outside Oaxaca; if unavailable, increase mulato and pasilla)
    *   2-3 chile costeño (or other mild, fruity dried chile)
*   **Aromatics & Spices (the soul):**
    *   1 large white onion, roughly chopped
    *   6-8 cloves garlic, peeled
    *   1 tablespoon black peppercorns
    *   1 teaspoon whole cloves
    *   1 teaspoon cumin seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon coriander seeds
    *   2-3 star anise pods
    *   2-inch cinnamon stick (Mexican canela preferred)
    *   A pinch of dried thyme and marjoram
*   **Nuts & Seeds (for body and richness):**
    *   1/2 cup raw almonds
    *   1/2 cup raw peanuts
    *   1/4 cup pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
    *   1/4 cup sesame seeds
*   **Thickeners & Sweeteners (for texture and balance):**
    *   2-3 ripe plantains, sliced
    *   1/2 cup raisins
    *   1/4 cup pitted prunes
    *   2 stale tortillas, torn
    *   1 slice stale bread (bolillo or similar)
*   **The Finishing Touches:**
    *   100-150g Mexican chocolate (Abuelita or Ibarra brands are common), roughly chopped
    *   1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil (for frying the paste)
    *   6-8 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian version)
    *   Salt to taste
    *   Optional: a pinch of sugar, a splash of apple cider vinegar for balance

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Mole Negro

Preparing Oaxacan Black Mole is a multi-step process that requires patience and dedication, but each step contributes to the final, unforgettable flavor.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully toast all dried chiles on a comal or dry skillet over medium-low heat until fragrant and slightly puffed (about 30 seconds per side). Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. Once toasted, remove the stems and seeds. Place the toasted chiles in a large bowl, cover with boiling water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until softened. Drain, reserving some of the soaking liquid.
2.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds & Spices:** In a separate dry skillet, toast the almonds, peanuts, pumpkin seeds, and sesame seeds separately until fragrant and lightly browned. Set aside. Next, lightly toast the peppercorns, cloves, cumin, coriander, star anise, and cinnamon stick until aromatic. Set aside with the nuts and seeds.
3.  **Fry Aromatics & Thickeners:** Heat a tablespoon of lard or oil in a large skillet. Fry the chopped onion and garlic until softened and lightly caramelized. Add the sliced plantains and fry until golden brown. Then, fry the raisins, prunes, torn tortillas, and stale bread until lightly crisped.
4.  **Char Select Ingredients (for the black color):** A crucial step for Mole Negro is to char some ingredients. Take a few of the rehydrated chiles, a quarter of the onion, and a couple of garlic cloves and char them directly over an open flame or under a broiler until very dark, almost black, but not completely burnt. This contributes to the mole's distinctive color and smoky depth.
5.  **Blend Everything into a Paste:** Combine the rehydrated chiles (and charred chiles), toasted nuts and seeds, toasted spices, fried aromatics, fried plantains, fruits, tortillas, and bread in a blender. Add some chicken broth and a little reserved chile soaking liquid. Blend in batches, adding more liquid as needed, until you achieve a very smooth, thick paste. This might take several minutes per batch. For the smoothest mole, you can pass the paste through a fine-mesh sieve or chino.
6.  **Cook the Mole:** Heat the remaining lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the mole paste into the hot fat. This step, known as "sazonar," is vital for developing the mole's flavor. Stir constantly for at least 20-30 minutes, allowing the paste to fry and deepen in color. It will thicken considerably.
7.  **Simmer and Finish:** Gradually add the chicken broth to the mole paste, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer. Add the chopped Mexican chocolate and stir until it completely melts and is incorporated. Continue to simmer the mole over low heat for at least 1-2 hours, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken to your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add more broth.
8.  **Season:** Taste and adjust seasoning with salt, and if desired, a pinch of sugar or a splash of apple cider vinegar to balance the flavors.

## Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

Crafting an exceptional Oaxacan Black Mole is a journey, and these tips will help ensure your efforts yield delicious results:

*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** This is the golden rule. Charring is different from burning. Burnt chiles will impart an acrid, bitter taste to your mole that is impossible to correct. Toast them gently until aromatic.
*   **The Power of Sazonar:** The step where you fry the mole paste in hot lard or oil is non-negotiable. This process "cooks" the raw flavors out of the chiles and spices, deepening the color and building the foundational flavor profile of your Oaxacan Black Mole.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A truly great mole has a luxurious, velvety texture. Invest time in blending, using a high-powered blender, and consider straining the finished paste to remove any stubborn bits of chile skin or seeds.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** Mole Negro is not a quick meal. The long simmering time allows all the diverse flavors to meld and harmonize. Don't rush it. The longer it simmers, the richer and more complex it becomes.
*   **Taste, Taste, Taste:** Seasoning is crucial. Taste your mole throughout the simmering process and adjust salt, sweetness, or a touch of acidity (like apple cider vinegar) until the balance is perfect.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole Negro often tastes even better the next day, as the flavors continue to develop. It also freezes beautifully, making it an excellent candidate for meal prep.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Once your magnificent Oaxacan Black Mole is complete, the serving possibilities are endless, though traditionally it graces special occasions.

*   **With Poultry:** The most classic pairing is with shredded chicken or turkey. Simply warm your cooked protein and generously spoon the mole over it.
*   **Enmoladas:** Use the mole to make enchiladas, dipping tortillas in the warm sauce, filling them with shredded chicken or cheese, rolling them up, and topping with more mole.
*   **Pork or Beef:** Mole Negro also pairs wonderfully with slow-cooked pork shoulder or beef short ribs.
*   **Vegetarian Delights:** For a vegetarian option, serve the mole over roasted vegetables (like sweet potatoes, cauliflower, or plantains), with cheesy tamales, or simply alongside fluffy white rice and warm corn tortillas.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, a few sprigs of fresh cilantro, or thinly sliced red onion add both visual appeal and a textural contrast.
*   **Accompaniments:** Always serve with warm corn tortillas for scooping up every last drop, and a side of simple white rice to soak up the rich sauce.

## Variations and Substitutions

While Oaxacan Black Mole has a distinct profile, some ingredient adjustments can be made depending on availability and personal preference.

*   **Chiles:** If chilhuacle negro chiles are impossible to find, increase the quantities of mulato and pasilla chiles to compensate for the depth and color. For less heat, you can remove more seeds from the chiles.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** While almonds, peanuts, pumpkin, and sesame seeds are traditional, you can experiment with pecans or walnuts, though this will alter the flavor slightly.
*   **Chocolate:** Authentic Mexican chocolate is best for its specific blend of cacao, sugar, and often cinnamon. If unavailable, a good quality dark chocolate (70% cacao or higher) with a pinch of cinnamon can be used. Avoid overly sweet chocolate.
*   **Broth:** Chicken broth is standard, but a rich vegetable broth works perfectly for a vegetarian version.
*   **Fat:** Lard contributes a traditional richness, but any neutral vegetable oil (like canola or grapeseed oil) can be used.
*   **Sweetness/Acidity:** Some recipes might call for a small amount of piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or another unrefined sugar, and a touch of vinegar or tomatillo for a subtle acidic balance. Adjust these to your liking.

The creation of Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is an act of culinary devotion, resulting in a dish that is deeply embedded in Mexican culture and flavor. It’s a rewarding challenge for any home cook looking to explore the depths of authentic Mexican dinner recipes. While it demands time and effort, the unparalleled richness and complexity of this magnificent sauce make every moment spent in the kitchen utterly worthwhile. Prepare to be amazed by the "king of moles."
